text: thank you for joining for another robotic surgery demonstration today we're talking about robotic cholecystectomy this is actually a recent case i performed quite an interesting finding on ct scan a seven-year-old woman with chronic gi symptoms who had been worked up by her primary doctor eventually had a ct scan which demonstrated this massively distended gallbladder full of stones measuring up to three centimeters Gallbladder was actually compressing upon the stomach and was felt to be the source of her chronic, decades-long GI symptoms. So we set up for a robotic case. We told the patient and had a thorough conversation that we would anticipate a much larger extraction site than usual, and we may modulate the site of the extraction. But we started with our standard robotic setup.

The surgeon uses an extra five millimeter port in the patient's left upper quadrant for additional manipulation of the distended gallbladder. Due to the large size of the stones, a medial-first dissection approach is employed, described as 'opening up the book.' The gallbladder is being mobilized off the cystic plate laterally, indicating the need for an extraction incision.

The surgeon completes the mobilization of the gallbladder, dissecting it off the liver from medial to lateral. The cystic artery is clipped and transected, preparing for the gallbladder's externalization through a mini laparotomy.

The surgeon makes a five-centimeter incision in the right subcostal area for gallbladder extraction. The gallbladder is opened outside the body, decompressed, and filleted open down to the cystic duct orifice, where it is transected and removed without spillage. The incision is closed in layers using a small bite technique.
